comment: Uses indexing and retrieval/generation but does not match the canonical component list and overweights RAG agents.
Covers indexing, retrieval, and generation with citations that support the main claims.
Covers embeddings, vector store, retriever, and model, which satisfies the component-count requirement.
Names the four expected RAGAS metrics, but citations mainly support the metric names rather than the full definitions.
Answers the metric names and gives reasonable explanations, but the cited table only partially supports those explanations.
Refuses despite retrieving the metric table; the citation is related but does not support the refusal.
Mentions low-level infrastructure and integration but misses the stateful-agent focus and clear LangChain dependency relation.
Correctly describes persistent/stateful agent orchestration and the LangChain/LangGraph relationship.
Treats the evidence as insufficient even though the retrieved chunks contain the required relationship.
Explains FAISS as a similarity-search library but does not clearly connect it to RAG vector indexing and retrieval.
Explains both FAISS's general purpose and its role as RAG vector-store/retrieval infrastructure.
Clearly ties FAISS to vector storage and retrieval of relevant document embeddings in RAG.
Correctly identifies Self-RAG and self-reflection, but the cited evidence only partly supports the mechanism detail.
Correctly identifies Self-RAG and explains reflection tokens and adaptive retrieval.
Answers Agentic RAG instead of the target Self-RAG paper, so both answer and citations miss the intended method.
Lists reflection, planning, tool use, and multi-agent collaboration with direct survey support.
Covers the expected Agentic RAG directions and adds workflow/taxonomy framing.
Refuses the question even though the retrieved survey chunks contain the required directions.
Only recommends RAGFlow, far below the minimum project list required by the rubric.
Recommends two projects, but the list is incomplete and one item is outside the target four-project set.
Fails to provide a recommendation list and incorrectly treats the available evidence as insufficient.
Avoids an absolute claim and mentions Self-RAG, but does not compare three methods by scenario or mechanism.
Cautiously compares Self-RAG and A-RAG, but CRAG and scenario-level tradeoffs remain underdeveloped.
Gives a cautious no-universal-winner answer, but does not explain the expected method-specific strengths.
Forms a partial contrast between agentic orchestration and data/context focus, but not the full design comparison.
Gives useful positioning differences, but still lacks the precise chain-composition versus index-centric comparison.
Refuses the comparison and does not use the available LlamaIndex evidence productively.
Correctly contrasts Self-RAG self-reflection with CRAG corrective post-processing.
Clearly explains Self-RAG reflection tokens, CRAG document filtering, and the internal-versus-external distinction.
Refuses the comparison despite retrieving relevant Self-RAG and CRAG chunks.
Identifies both platforms at a high level but does not fully state the Dify application-platform versus RAGFlow engine distinction.
Clearly distinguishes Dify as a broader LLM application platform and RAGFlow as a RAG-focused engine/context layer.
Refuses the comparison despite retrieving Dify and RAGFlow evidence.
Retrieves the two paper records but does not produce the required classification-perspective comparison.
Identifies one survey's taxonomy dimensions but cannot complete the contrast with the SoK paper.
Fails to answer the paper-comparison question and cites chunks that do not support a classification contrast.
Does not perform the allowed manual classification of the paper list, so the type-distribution task remains unfinished.
Again refuses to classify the paper list and therefore misses the core requirement.
Still does not classify the ten-paper list; context expansion does not solve this aggregation task.
Finds only a small subset of the expected RAG improvement directions.
Lists several directions, but several are generic categories rather than the expected paper-specific method families.
Refuses to summarize the improvement directions despite retrieving relevant paper-list material.
Correctly classifies three projects but leaves Dify unresolved, so the four-project classification is incomplete.
Classifies Dify/RAGFlow as RAG platforms and CrewAI/LangGraph as agent frameworks with usable evidence.
Refuses the classification despite retrieving evidence for the relevant projects.
Computes the Naive RAG and Agentic RAG averages and the 0.1025 difference correctly.
Computes all metric deltas and the average difference correctly.
Refuses even though the retrieved table contains the necessary values.
Identifies arXiv API and Fetcher but misses the direct urllib request path required for full credit.
Captures API and Fetcher evidence, but does not clearly demonstrate the third acquisition method with an example.
Refuses the code-analysis question despite retrieving crawler-code chunks.
Infers save_md parameters and some metadata, but misses part of the function evidence and metadata coverage.
Correctly identifies save_md parameters, function, title/source/time metadata, and content writing.
Refuses the function-analysis task despite retrieving the relevant save_md chunk.
Computes the C0-to-C2 metric increases correctly and reports the largest gain as context precision/context recall.
Correctly calculates the increases and identifies context precision/context recall as the largest gains.
Refuses despite retrieving the metric table needed for the calculation.
Correctly gives an evidence-bounded conclusion and concrete reasons against universal superiority.
Correctly states that current evidence is insufficient for a universal superiority claim and explains why.
Reaches the cautious conclusion, but the reasons are weaker and less tied to the strongest limitation evidence.
